title: Tags and release points

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Tags and release points; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A tag gives a stable human-readable reference to a specific Git object, commonly a release commit; annotated tags also store message, tagger, and optional signature.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An annotated v1.0.0 tag points to the tested release commit and records release context before that tag is pushed explicitly. Your goal is to create, inspect and verify a version tag on the intended commit. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Selective staging and first commit; Log, show and graph. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A tag gives a stable human-readable reference to a specific Git object, commonly a release commit; annotated tags also store message, tagger, and optional signature. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Tags can be moved or deleted, lightweight and annotated tags differ, and creating a local tag does not automatically publish it to a remote. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: A tag is a read-only branch that automatically follows new commits and appears on every remote.

includes: Use annotated tags to name reviewed immutable release commits.
Create, inspect and verify a version tag on the intended commit.
excludes: Tags can be moved or deleted, lightweight and annotated tags differ, and creating a local tag does not automatically publish it to a remote.

outcomes: Create, inspect and verify a version tag on the intended commit.

examples.title: Tags and release points: direct case
examples.situation: An annotated v1.0.0 tag points to the tested release commit and records release context before that tag is pushed explicitly.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: An annotated v1.0.0 tag points to the tested release commit and records release context before that tag is pushed explicitly.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Treat a tag as a named pointer to one chosen object, verify its target, and publish or protect it according to project policy.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Treat a tag as a named pointer to one chosen object, verify its target, and publish or protect it according to project policy.

examples.title: Tags and release points: boundary case
examples.situation: A deployment resolves the tag's commit identity rather than assuming a same-named branch or latest main commit.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A deployment resolves the tag's commit identity rather than assuming a same-named branch or latest main commit.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Tags can be moved or deleted, lightweight and annotated tags differ, and creating a local tag does not automatically publish it to a remote.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: A tag is a read-only branch that automatically follows new commits and appears on every remote.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Create, inspect and verify a version tag on the intended commit.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A tag is a read-only branch that automatically follows new commits and appears on every remote.
misconceptions.correction: Treat a tag as a named pointer to one chosen object, verify its target, and publish or protect it according to project policy.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: A tag is a read-only branch that automatically follows new commits and appears on every remote.

analogy.example: A signed lab notebook can model Tags and release points: stable entries record why a change happened, named markers identify milestones, and comparisons expose differences.
usefulFor: Inspecting immutable records, messages, selected versions, differences, and milestone names
limitations: Git history is a directed graph with movable references, merges, and content-addressed objects, not a single bound notebook.

recap.summary: You should now be able to create, inspect and verify a version tag on the intended commit. Keep this boundary in view: Tags can be moved or deleted, lightweight and annotated tags differ, and creating a local tag does not automatically publish it to a remote.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Tags and release points without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: A tag is a read-only branch that automatically follows new commits and appears on every remote.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A deployment resolves the tag's commit identity rather than assuming a same-named branch or latest main commit.
